creator: h@hartzer.sh
text:
I wanted to say that I appreciate all of the help here. This has been a very interesting issue. I'm glad that at least the top reporting bug was caught, and is now in 14.2.

Speaking of 14.2, I finally upgraded. Lately, I've been on a somewhat older system with 8GB of memory.

Unfortunately, I should have tested the 14.2 betas/RCs beforehand -- on my hardware, for whatever reason, I can have SMP disabled or 8GB of memory, but not both (bug #285867.) With SMP disabled the system is particularly slow and there's some weird bugs present that don't happen when SMP is on (bug #286018.) I'm now limited to 4GB of memory if I override it in the loader.conf, or 2GB of memory if I use sticks without an override (this is a real mystery to me.) 

Even with 4GB of memory, Firefox is just unusable. I'll have maybe 4 or 5 tabs open and start to OOM. Now the OOM behavior has worked pretty well, so no issues there. But without changing hardware, FreeBSD is no longer a viable operating system for desktop use.

I decided to give OpenBSD a try and things have pretty much "just worked" across the board. 7.6 booted with SMP and 8GB without any problems. Firefox functioned great. For curiousity's sake, I went down to 2GB of memory and disabled swap. Firefox functioned maybe a hair better on OpenBSD with 2GB of memory than FreeBSD with 4GB of memory. And its OOM worked great, rather fast actually. The OpenBSD scheduler was also far more responsive for me.

I then tried 2GB with swap on OpenBSD, and Firefox was usable, albeit slow. I also disabled SMP and tried to reproduce the most obvious bug I had with FreeBSD and SMP disabled, and I could not (turning off the display leading to a hang.)

Thus, I feel it's probably time to jump ship. I don't have any large ZFS systems, 40Gbit/sec NICs, 1TB of memory, or anything that really warrants FreeBSD's extra capabilities. But I don't take this lightly, I'm rather attached to FreeBSD and its community. I like the development process and being able to get in a few small patches. I deeply appreciate that it's versioned with git! But I'd like to use my 15 year old laptops and I'm not sure how to do that any more with FreeBSD.

Anyway, again, Mark and Mark, I appreciate your help and time.
